title: Scaling AI Inference Across Multiple GPUs Using NVIDIA TensorRT with Multi-Device Inference Support
gist: NVIDIA TensorRT 11.0 introduces native multi-GPU inference via NCCL-based distributed collectives, enabling generative AI pipelines to scale beyond single-GPU memory limits. The release supports tensor parallelism and context parallelism strategies (AllGather KV, Ring Attention, DeepSpeed Ulysses), with benchmarks on Cosmos 3 and FLUX.1 showing DeepSpeed Ulysses delivers the lowest latency for long-sequence diffusion workloads at scale.

insights.claim: NVIDIA TensorRT 11.0 introduces native multi-GPU inference support, allowing a single neural network to execute across multiple GPUs through integrated NCCL distributed communication primitives, targeting production edge deployments.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: TensorRT is now a first-class multi-GPU inference framework, reducing developer friction and narrowing the gap with training-focused distributed systems.
insights.quote: Multi-device inference support, a new feature introduced in TensorRT 11.0, brings native high-performance multi-GPU inference to the TensorRT runtime, enabling multi-device production deployments targeting edge devices.

insights.claim: TensorRT 11.0 supports the full set of NVIDIA NCCL distributed collectives—AllReduce, Broadcast, Reduce, AllGather, ReduceScatter, AlltoAll, Gather, and Scatter—providing flexibility for diverse distributed inference architectures.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: Complete collective coverage means TensorRT can support arbitrary parallelism patterns, not just limited point-to-point communication, enabling more sophisticated distributed deployments.
insights.quote: The new multi-device feature covers the full set of NVIDIA NCCL distributed collectives: AllReduce, Broadcast, Reduce, AllGather, ReduceScatter, AlltoAll, Gather, and Scatter.
insights.locator: NVIDIA NCCL: The transport layer for distributed inference

insights.claim: Combining TensorRT 11.0 with Torch-TensorRT lets developers convert and deploy massive PyTorch models out-of-framework, removing single-device memory and compute constraints while retaining PyTorch as the model development environment.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: The PyTorch-to-TensorRT pipeline now scales to multi-GPU inference without requiring framework changes, lowering the barrier for production deployment of large models.
insights.quote: Combining the multi-device inference support in TensorRT with Torch-TensorRT, developers can convert and deploy massive PyTorch models out-of-framework, shattering single-device memory and compute limits.

insights.claim: NVIDIA NCCL automatically selects the optimal transport (NVLink, NVSwitch, PCIe, or InfiniBand) for a given topology, and TensorRT inherits this transport optimization for inference workloads by integrating directly with NCCL.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: Inference workloads now benefit from the same interconnect optimization that makes NVIDIA's training clusters efficient, strengthening NVIDIA's infrastructure lock-in across both training and inference.
insights.quote: NCCL automatically selects the optimal transport for a given topology, abstracting NVIDIA NVLink, NVIDIA NVSwitch, PCIe, and InfiniBand behind a uniform interface.
insights.locator: NVIDIA NCCL: The transport layer for distributed inference

themes.theme: Tensor Parallelism vs. Context Parallelism Trade-offs

insights.claim: Tensor parallelism partitions layer weights across GPUs and is the natural choice when a single layer's weights exceed single-GPU memory, with transformer blocks needing only one AllReduce per block to bound communication overhead.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: Understanding that tensor parallelism is weight-memory-bound while context parallelism is sequence-length-bound helps architects choose the right strategy for their inference bottleneck.
insights.quote: This reduces per-device memory weight, making it the natural (and often the only) choice when an individual layer's weights exceed the memory of a single GPU, independent of the input sequence length or batch size.

insights.claim: Context parallelism partitions the input sequence across GPUs and is especially effective for long-sequence workloads where attention's quadratic scaling dominates compute and memory, and is a natural fit for diffusion and DiT models with bidirectional attention.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: Diffusion model operators building multi-GPU inference pipelines should prioritize context parallelism, not tensor parallelism, as it directly targets the attention bottleneck dominant in these workloads.
insights.quote: It is also an especially natural fit for diffusion and DiT models, whose bidirectional attention sidesteps the load-imbalance issues that arise with causal masks.

insights.claim: AllGather KV context parallelism exchanges key and value shards across ranks before computing local attention, costing one additional collective per attention block while shrinking the local Q×Kᵀ matrix multiplication proportionally to the number of ranks.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: AllGather KV provides a simple baseline for multi-GPU attention but increases memory pressure since full K and V tensors must be materialized on each GPU.
insights.quote: A straightforward way to implement context parallelism is the AllGather KV approach, where ranks exchange their key (K) and value (V) shards through an AllGather collective before computing local attention.

insights.claim: Ring Attention overlaps communication and computation by streaming K and V in a ring topology while each GPU processes its local Q simultaneously, and reduces memory footprint by using online softmax to avoid materializing full K and V tensors on any GPU.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: Ring Attention offers a memory-efficient alternative to AllGather KV for very long sequences where full K/V materialization is infeasible, at the cost of increased implementation complexity.
insights.quote: Ring Attention, where communication and computation are overlapped. This enables each GPU to process its local Q simultaneously as the K and V continuously stream past in a ring topology.

insights.claim: DeepSpeed Ulysses uses two all-to-all collectives per attention block—one to redistribute full-sequence Q/K/V per attention head subset, one to gather results—enabling parallel head computation with only O(heads/ranks) local compute per GPU.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: DeepSpeed Ulysses trades two all-to-all communications for the ability to compute attention heads in parallel, which benchmarks show makes it the winner for long-sequence diffusion workloads.
insights.quote: Before the attention computation, it employs an all-to-all communication collective on the partitioned Q, K, and V. This ensures that each GPU receives the full sequence length, but only for a non-overlapping subset of the attention heads, enabling them to compute attention in parallel.

themes.theme: Benchmark Results: Cosmos 3 and FLUX.1 Pipelines

insights.claim: Benchmarks on NVIDIA Cosmos 3 video generation show DeepSpeed Ulysses is the clear winner for diffusion models with excessively long context lengths (tens of thousands of input tokens), delivering the lowest end-to-end latency across strategies on up to 8 GPUs.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: Operators deploying NVIDIA Cosmos or similar video generation models at scale should default to DeepSpeed Ulysses for context parallelism rather than AllGather KV or Ring Attention.
insights.quote: Based on these benchmarks, Ulysses is the clear winner when a diffusion model has excessively long context lengths (in the order of tens of thousands of input tokens).

insights.claim: Benchmarks on FLUX.1 image generation confirm DeepSpeed Ulysses as the lowest-latency context parallelism strategy, while Ring Attention also scaled well to 4 GPUs, offering a valid alternative for smaller multi-GPU configurations.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: For image generation workloads at 4-GPU scale, Ring Attention remains competitive with Ulysses and may be preferable for memory-constrained deployments.
insights.quote: Based on the benchmarks, the Ulysses strategy is the winner in the case of image generation as well, but it's worth noting that Ring Attention also scaled well to 4 GPUs.

insights.claim: The benchmarks for Cosmos 3 and FLUX.1 were run on a single node with 8 GPUs and models were first authored in PyTorch then converted using Torch-TensorRT, validating the PyTorch-to-C++ production deployment workflow.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: The Torch-TensorRT conversion path to C++ is now production-validated for multi-GPU media generation, reducing the risk for teams considering this deployment architecture.
insights.quote: These pipelines were first authored in PyTorch, then converted out of the framework using Torch-TensorRT to produce NVIDIA TensorRT engines suitable for deployment in C++ inference applications.
insights.locator: Benchmarks: Media generation with context parallelism in C++

themes.theme: Generative AI Workload Memory Scaling Challenge

insights.claim: Generative AI inference workloads are rapidly outgrowing the memory and compute budget of single GPUs, creating a structural need for multi-device inference that preserves TensorRT's optimizations like kernel fusions, memory planning, and quantization.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: The memory scaling constraint is a durable hardware bottleneck that drives demand for multi-GPU inference infrastructure, benefiting NVIDIA's NVLink and NVSwitch interconnect business.
insights.quote: Generative AI workloads are rapidly outgrowing the memory and compute budget of single GPUs. For inference developers building media generation pipelines, the challenge is scaling across multiple devices without sacrificing the critical optimizations.

insights.claim: Diffusion-based image and video generation pipelines concentrate most compute and memory usage inside attention blocks operating over sequences of tens of thousands of tokens, where attention's quadratic scaling with sequence length makes it the dominant bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: Attention is the critical path for multi-GPU scaling of diffusion models, meaning any GPU architecture improvements to attention throughput (e.g., FlashAttention integration) will have outsized impact on media generation latency.
insights.quote: A high-resolution image latent or a multi-frame video clip can produce sequences of tens of thousands of tokens per block, and attention scales quadratically with sequence length.

themes.theme: NVIDIA Cosmos 3 World Foundation Model Platform

insights.claim: NVIDIA Cosmos 3 Nano is a world foundation model capable of generating images, video, audio, and other formats from multimodal inputs including text, images, and video, positioning it as a general-purpose generative media platform.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: Cosmos 3's multimodal output capability—covering images, video, and audio—broadens NVIDIA's competitive surface in generative AI beyond pure language models, entering direct competition with OpenAI Sora and similar video generation platforms.
insights.quote: The NVIDIA Cosmos model platform is a world foundation model platform, and the Cosmos3-Nano model can generate images, video, audio, and other formats based on multimodal inputs, including text, images, and video.

insights.claim: TensorRT multi-device inference requires each MPI rank to independently select its local CUDA device, initialize NCCL, create its own TensorRT engine, and create its own execution context, with the NCCL communicator attached per context and valid for its lifetime.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: The rank-local engine design means engine building is embarrassingly parallel and engines do not need to be shared across GPUs, simplifying deployment orchestration at the cost of per-rank memory for engine copies.
insights.quote: Each rank selects its local CUDA device, initializes NCCL, creates its own TensorRT engine, creates its own execution context, and attaches the NCCL communicator.

insights.claim: TensorRT 11.0 multi-device inference is launched via OpenMPI (mpirun), enabling standard HPC job scheduling and process management tooling to orchestrate multi-GPU inference deployments without custom infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Peter Kisfaludi
insights.so_what: OpenMPI integration lowers operational complexity for enterprises already running HPC infrastructure, facilitating faster adoption of multi-GPU TensorRT inference in data center environments.
insights.quote: Run the application with OpenMPI on 8 GPUs.
